title: Tanacetum cinerariifolium
taxonomy: Tanacetum cinerariifolium is classified as a flowering plant belonging to the family Asteraceae. This daisy-like plant is commonly known for its use in cosmetics due to its pyrethrin content. It is native to Europe, Asia, and Western Siberia, and has been naturalized in other parts of the world. The plant's classification further breaks down to the genus Tanacetum, which includes many species of tansy and related plants. Tanacetum cinerariifolium is also known as pyrethrum daisy.
uses: Tanacetum cinerariifolium, also known as pyrethrum, has been used in traditional medicine for its anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties. Its flowers, in particular, contain pyrethrin, a compound that has been shown to have insecticidal and antiparasitic effects. In some cultures, the plant is used to treat fever, skin conditions, and respiratory issues. Additionally, pyrethrum has been used as a natural remedy for insect bites and stings. The antiseptic properties of Tanacetum cinerariifolium make it a popular ingredient in herbal remedies for wound care and skin health.
benefits: Tanacetum cinerariifolium, also known as Pyrethrum or Dalmatian chamomile, has been used for centuries due to its numerous health benefits. The plant's extracts have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, making it effective in treating various skin conditions, such as acne and eczema. The pyrethrin content in Tanacetum cinerariifolium has been used to repel insects and as an insecticide. Additionally, the plant is known to have antiseptic and antifungal properties, which can help in wound healing and reducing the risk of infections. Overall, Tanacetum cinerariifolium is considered a valuable natural remedy for promoting overall health and well-being.
properties: Tanacetum cinerariifolium, commonly known as tansy, has been used in traditional medicine for its various therapeutic properties. It is believed to have anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties, making it useful for treating wounds and skin conditions. The plant's essential oil has also been shown to have antibacterial and antifungal properties, which can help to combat infections. Additionally, tansy has been used to treat digestive issues, such as bloating and gas, due to its carminative properties. However, it is essential to note that tansy can be toxic in large quantities, and its use should be approached with caution.
constituents: Tanacetum cinerariifolium, commonly known as pyrethrum or daisy, contains a group of compounds known as pyrethrins as its active constituents. These pyrethrins are a mixture of esters of chrysanthemic acid and have insecticidal properties. They are responsible for the plant's ability to repel and kill insects, and are often used in insecticides and pest control products. The main components of pyrethrins include pyrethrin I, pyrethrin II, and cinerin I and II. Pyrethrins work by disrupting the insect nervous system, leading to paralysis and eventual death.
parts: Tanacetum cinerariifolium, also known as pyrethrum, is a flowering plant whose medicinal parts are primarily derived from its flowers and leaves. The flowers contain pyrethrin, a natural insecticide that is used to repel mosquitoes and other insects. The leaves of the plant also contain pyrethrin, but to a lesser extent than the flowers. The roots of the plant can be used to make a medicinal tea that is believed to have anti-inflammatory properties. However, further research is needed to confirm the efficacy of the roots as a medicinal part.
preparations: Tanacetum cinerariifolium, also known as Pyrethrum or Dalmatian chamomile, is used in herbal preparations for its potent insecticidal properties. The flowers of the plant are used to create a natural insecticide, often in the form of a powder or oil. This herbal preparation is commonly used in organic gardening and agriculture to control pests without harming the environment. The pyrethrins extracted from Tanacetum cinerariifolium are also used in commercial insecticides, albeit in a more refined and concentrated form. However, due to its potential toxicity to humans and other non-target organisms, the use of Tanacetum cinerariifolium preparations is typically limited to outdoor applications.
side_effects: Tanacetum cinerariifolium, also known as pyrethrum daisy or red daisy, can cause skin irritation, allergic reactions, and eye problems in some individuals due to its pyrethrins content. These compounds can also be toxic to cats and other pets if ingested. The plant's essential oil may cause respiratory issues and headaches in people who are sensitive to it. Pyrethrum daisy is generally considered non-toxic to humans, but large amounts of the plant or prolonged exposure can cause systemic toxicity. People should handle the plant with care and avoid inhaling its dust or essential oil.
